past_medical_history: Remote COVID-19 infection 18 months ago, mild course with full recovery
family_history: No family history of epilepsy, neurological disease, or psychiatric illness

social_history.living_situation: Lives in university dormitory with roommate

neurological_exam.mental_status: Somnolent, difficult to arouse. GCS 9 (E2 V3 M4). Opens eyes to painful stimuli. Verbalizations limited to incomprehensible sounds and occasional moaning. Does not follow commands. Unable to assess orientation, attention, or memory due to severely altered level of consciousness. During brief periods of relative alertness, displays purposeless agitation with repetitive vocalizations.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3 mm bilaterally. Extraocular movements appear intact on oculocephalic reflex testing. Corneal reflexes present bilaterally. Facial sensation intact to nailbed pressure. Mild symmetric facial hypotonia noted. Gag reflex intact. Tongue midline. No overt cranial nerve palsies identified. No meningeal signs (Kernig and Brudzinski negative).
neurological_exam.motor: Symmetric low muscle tone in all four extremities. Withdraws symmetrically to noxious stimuli in upper and lower extremities. No focal weakness appreciated. No decorticate or decerebrate posturing. Intermittent involuntary choreiform movements of bilateral upper extremities observed during examination.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws symmetrically to painful stimuli in all four extremities. Detailed sensory examination not possible due to altered consciousness.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. Symmetric throughout.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to assess volitional coordination testing due to altered mental status.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to assess; patient is bed-bound due to severely altered consciousness.
neurological_exam.additional: Two brief episodes of generalized stiffening with tonic extension lasting approximately 30-40 seconds each witnessed in the emergency department, followed by post-ictal unresponsiveness. Orofacial dyskinesias with repetitive lip-smacking and jaw movements observed intermittently. Episodes of autonomic instability including transient tachycardia to 120 bpm and blood pressure fluctuations from 100/60 to 145/90 mmHg observed during the examination period. Perioral cyanosis noted during one tonic episode before supplemental oxygen applied.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent seizures and altered consciousness following two days of abdominal symptoms and low-grade fever
patient.history_present_illness: This 19-year-old right-handed male university student was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his roommate witnessed two generalized tonic-clonic seizures over a 48-hour period. According to the roommate and the patient's parents who were contacted by phone, the patient had been complaining of abdominal pain and nausea approximately four days prior to presentation. Two days before admission, he developed a low-grade temperature of 37.5 degrees Celsius, vomited once, and appeared unusually fatigued but attributed his symptoms to a stomach virus. He missed two days of classes, which was unusual for him. The following day, his roommate heard rhythmic banging sounds from the patient's bed at approximately 2 AM and found him having a generalized tonic-clonic seizure with limb stiffening, rhythmic jerking, and urinary incontinence, lasting approximately two minutes. The patient was drowsy and confused afterward but appeared to recover over several hours, though he remained uncharacteristically quiet and had difficulty following conversations. His roommate noted that he seemed 'spaced out' and was making unusual repetitive lip-smacking movements that were not present previously. The next morning, approximately 22 hours after the first event, the patient had a second generalized seizure witnessed by his roommate, this time lasting nearly three minutes with post-ictal confusion lasting over an hour. Emergency medical services were called. The paramedics administered intravenous midazolam 5 mg for seizure management en route. On arrival, the patient was somnolent with a Glasgow Coma Scale of 9, displayed intermittent orofacial dyskinesias, and had markedly decreased oxygen saturation at 71% on room air with perioral cyanosis, which improved to 94% with supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula at 4 liters per minute. His parents reported that he had no prior seizure history, no psychiatric history, no recent head trauma, no recent travel to tick-endemic areas, and no known drug use. They noted he had a confirmed COVID-19 infection approximately 18 months ago with a mild course and full recovery, and that SARS-CoV-2 IgG antibodies were found positive on a subsequent check. His mother has autoimmune thyroiditis, and his maternal grandmother has rheumatoid arthritis.

background.continuity: Continuous with intermittent generalized voltage attenuation

findings.type: Focal intermittent theta and delta slowing
findings.frequency: 3-5 Hz theta intermixed with 1-2 Hz delta
findings.morphology: Semi-rhythmic, moderate to high amplitude (80-150 microvolts)
findings.state: Present during both wakefulness and drowsiness

findings.location: Generalized, maximal over frontocentral regions bilaterally
findings.frequency: 1-2 Hz delta activity with superimposed 20-25 Hz beta activity
findings.morphology: Rhythmic delta waves with overriding fast beta activity creating a brush-like pattern

findings.location: Left frontotemporal onset with secondary bilateral spread
findings.frequency: One electrographic seizure captured during the 40-minute recording, evolving from rhythmic theta to repetitive spike-and-wave complexes
findings.morphology: Rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta evolving to 2-3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges
findings.state: Occurred during apparent behavioral unresponsiveness with subtle oral automatisms

artifacts.description: Intermittent movement artifact related to orofacial dyskinesias and involuntary upper extremity movements
Brief episodes of diffuse myogenic artifact during tonic stiffening episodes

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient's inability to cooperate
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed; no photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Severely abnormal EEG demonstrating extreme delta brush pattern, focal left frontal intermittent theta-delta slowing, and one electrographic seizure of left frontotemporal onset. The overall pattern indicates diffuse cortical dysfunction with superimposed focal pathology and active epileptogenic activity. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Recording limited to 40 minutes; continuous EEG monitoring is strongly recommended to assess full seizure burden and guide management

findings.location: Bilateral hippocampi (medial temporal lobes), left greater than right
findings.size: Left hippocampus: diffuse involvement, Right hippocampus: focal 8 mm area

findings.location: Base of the right frontal lobe, inferior frontal gyrus

additional_observations: Ventricles normal in size and configuration
No acute hemorrhage or hemorrhagic transformation
Prior CT brain (outside hospital) reportedly normal
mri.impression: Bilateral hippocampal T2/FLAIR hyperintensity with associated cortical thickening (left greater than right) and a separate 10 mm FLAIR hyperintense focus at the base of the right frontal lobe. No restricted diffusion, no hemorrhage, no pathological enhancement. The absence of hemorrhagic changes and restricted diffusion makes HSV encephalitis less likely. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 113 bpm. No acute ischemic changes. Normal intervals. No arrhythmia.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 16.9 x10^9/L (H), Neutrophils 13.2 x10^9/L (H), Sodium 134 mEq/L (L), Bicarbonate 18 mEq/L (L), Glucose 124 mg/dL (H), pH 7.25  (L), pCO2 52 mmHg (H), pO2 72 mmHg (L), Lactate 8.1 mmol/L (H), SARS-CoV-2 IgG Positive  (H), CK 412 U/L (H).

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 18 cmH2O. WBC: 3 cells/uL (normal, no pleocytosis) (N/A PMN/85% lymph). Protein: 38 mg/dL. Glucose: 77 mg/dL (ratio 0.62 (serum glucose 124 mg/dL)). Gram stain: No organisms seen. Bacterial culture: No growth at 48 hours. HSV-1 PCR: Negative. HSV-2 PCR: Negative. HHV-6 PCR: Negative. HHV-7 PCR: Positive. HHV-8 PCR: Negative. VZV IgM: Negative. VZV IgG: Negative. Lyme IgM: Negative. Lyme IgG: Negative. Tick-borne encephalitis IgM: Negative. Tick-borne encephalitis IgG: Negative. IgG index: 0.58 (normal, <0.70). IgM (CSF): Normal. Albumin (CSF): Normal. Intrathecal IgG synthesis: Not detected. Oligoclonal bands: Not detected. Cytology: No atypical cells. Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Positive (titer 1:128).

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Serum anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:64)  (H), CSF anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:128)  (H).
Abnormal values: Anti-NMDA receptor Ab (CSF) Positive (1:128)  (H).

Testicular/Abdominal Ultrasound.value: Normal size (4.2 x 2.5 x 2.8 cm), homogeneous echotexture, normal vascularity on Doppler
Normal size (4.0 x 2.4 x 2.6 cm), homogeneous echotexture, normal vascularity on Doppler
Liver, spleen, kidneys, and pancreas normal in size and echotexture. No abdominal masses or lymphadenopathy.

output.impression: No evidence of occult malignancy on CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis. No masses, lymphadenopathy, or suspicious lesions identified. In male patients with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, tumors are identified in a minority of cases. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 bands, not present in serum)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 20 cmH2O. WBC: 28 cells/uL (elevated) (4% PMN/92% lymph). Protein: 62 mg/dL. Glucose: 65 mg/dL (ratio 0.60 (serum glucose 108 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 bands, not present in serum). IgG index: 0.78 (elevated, normal <0.70). Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Positive (titer 1:128, unchanged from prior). Cytology: Activated lymphocytes, no malignant cells.

output.query: HHV-7 encephalitis anti-NMDAR autoimmune encephalitis viral trigger

results.title: Human herpesvirus 7 and anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis: an emerging association
results.summary: Case series of 5 patients with anti-NMDAR encephalitis who had concurrent HHV-7 DNA detection in CSF. Authors hypothesize that HHV-7 reactivation may serve as an immunological trigger for NMDAR autoimmunity through molecular mimicry or bystander activation.

results.title: HHV-7 in cerebrospinal fluid: clinical significance and diagnostic challenges
results.summary: Review article noting that HHV-7 DNA is frequently detected in CSF of immunocompetent individuals (up to 5-10% of routine CSF samples). The clinical significance of isolated HHV-7 CSF positivity remains uncertain; it may represent latent virus reactivation rather than active HHV-7 encephalitis.

results.title: Post-infectious autoimmune encephalitis: viral triggers and immune mechanisms
results.summary: Comprehensive review discussing how viral infections (including herpesviruses, SARS-CoV-2, and others) can trigger autoimmune encephalitis via molecular mimicry, epitope spreading, or nonspecific immune activation. Anti-NMDAR encephalitis has been reported following HSV encephalitis, and emerging evidence suggests other herpesviruses including HHV-6 and HHV-7 may also serve as triggers.

output.summary: HHV-7 DNA detection in CSF may represent incidental shedding (found in 5-10% of routine samples) or a potential viral trigger for anti-NMDAR autoimmunity. Current evidence suggests herpesviruses can trigger autoimmune encephalitis through molecular mimicry. The finding of HHV-7 in this clinical context does not establish HHV-7 encephalitis as the primary diagnosis; the positive anti-NMDAR antibodies remain the definitive diagnostic finding.

output.proposed: IV methylprednisolone 1g daily x5 days + IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x5 days + levetiracetam 1000mg IV q12h
interactions: Methylprednisolone + IVIG: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction; commonly co-administered in autoimmune encephalitis
Methylprednisolone may increase blood glucose: monitor closely given patient's stress hyperglycemia
Levetiracetam: No significant interactions with methylprednisolone or IVIG
warnings: Monitor blood glucose closely with high-dose methylprednisolone
Monitor renal function with IVIG infusion; ensure adequate hydration
IVIG carries risk of thromboembolic events; monitor for DVT symptoms
Consider PPI or H2 blocker for gastroprotection during high-dose steroid therapy
output.formulary_status: All medications available on hospital formulary
alternatives: Plasma exchange (PLEX) may be used as alternative to or in combination with IVIG as first-line immunotherapy
If first-line fails: rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly x4 as second-line

differential.key_distinguishing: HSV encephalitis typically presents with unilateral temporal lobe involvement, hemorrhagic changes on MRI, high fever, and positive CSF HSV PCR. In this case, HSV-1/2 PCR is negative, MRI shows bilateral non-hemorrhagic changes, and CRP is normal.

HHV-7 DNA was detected in CSF, but isolated HHV-7 CSF positivity is found in 5-10% of routine samples and may represent incidental shedding. The clinical presentation with orofacial dyskinesias, autonomic instability, and extreme delta brush on EEG is far more consistent with anti-NMDAR encephalitis than primary viral encephalitis.

While seizures are prominent, EEG shows no primary epileptiform activity. The extreme delta brush pattern and the encephalopathic presentation with orofacial dyskinesias and autonomic instability point to an underlying encephalitis rather than a primary seizure disorder.

Patient has prior COVID-19 but current SARS-CoV-2 PCR is negative, CRP is normal, and the clinical presentation lacks the systemic inflammatory features (cardiac involvement, rash, gastrointestinal symptoms) required for MIS diagnosis criteria.

Paraneoplastic limbic encephalitis can present similarly but is typically associated with intracellular antibodies (Hu, Ma2, CV2). Anti-NMDAR antibodies are a cell-surface antibody. Imaging shows no occult malignancy. The positive anti-NMDAR antibodies confirm the specific diagnosis.

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize patient: secure airway, establish IV access, apply supplemental oxygen to correct hypoxemia, continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring. Administer IV benzodiazepine if active seizure.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improvement in oxygen saturation, seizure termination

optimal_actions.action: Obtain urgent labs: CBC, BMP, ABG, lactate, CRP, CK, liver function, thyroid function, urine drug screen, blood cultures, SARS-CoV-2 testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Neutrophilic leukocytosis, mixed acidosis with elevated lactate from seizures, mild hyponatremia, normal inflammatory markers, normal thyroid function, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hippocampal FLAIR hyperintensity and right frontal FLAIR focus consistent with limbic encephalitis

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with comprehensive CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, cultures, viral PCRs (HSV, VZV, HHV-6/7), Lyme and tick-borne encephalitis serologies, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No pleocytosis initially (can be normal early in course), negative viral PCRs except HHV-7, positive anti-NMDAR antibodies confirming diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h pending HSV PCR results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric coverage until HSV encephalitis excluded
Extreme delta brush pattern, focal slowing, electrographic seizures

optimal_actions.action: Initiate anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam 1000 mg IV q12h)
Once anti-NMDAR antibodies confirmed: initiate first-line immunotherapy with IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily x 5 days AND IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduction in antibody-mediated neuronal injury and clinical improvement

optimal_actions.action: Screen for underlying neoplasm: testicular/abdominal ultrasound and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No tumor identified (tumors less common in male patients with anti-NMDAR encephalitis)

optimal_actions.action: Admit to ICU or step-down unit for close monitoring of autonomic instability, seizures, and respiratory status
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Safe monitoring environment with access to advanced airway management

optimal_actions.action: If insufficient clinical improvement after 2 weeks of first-line immunotherapy: escalate to second-line therapy with rituximab
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical improvement with B-cell depletion if first-line inadequate

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
Start empiric IV acyclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IV steroids and IVIG or plasma exchange) once diagnosis confirmed
Screen for underlying tumor with appropriate imaging
Initiate anti-seizure medication for seizure control
Continuous EEG monitoring to assess subclinical seizure burden
Admit to ICU for monitoring of autonomic instability and respiratory status
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing as primary epilepsy without excluding autoimmune encephalitis
Treating HHV-7 CSF positivity as definitive HHV-7 encephalitis without considering autoimmune etiologies
Administering high-dose antipsychotics for behavioral symptoms, which can worsen dyskinesias
Delaying immunotherapy while waiting for antibody results if clinical suspicion is high
Performing brain biopsy when anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are positive
Rapid sodium correction if hyponatremia worsens (risk of osmotic demyelination)
Discharging after initial seizure without thorough workup for encephalitis
key_reasoning_points: New-onset seizures with altered consciousness, orofacial dyskinesias, and autonomic instability in a young patient should raise immediate suspicion for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, even in males where it is less common
Extreme delta brush on EEG is highly characteristic of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and can help guide early clinical suspicion before antibody results return
Normal initial CSF (no pleocytosis) does not exclude anti-NMDAR encephalitis; up to 20% of cases have normal CSF early in the course, and repeat LP may show evolving pleocytosis

HHV-7 DNA detection in CSF is often incidental (found in 5-10% of routine CSF samples) and should not distract from the autoimmune diagnosis when clinical features and antibodies point to anti-NMDAR encephalitis
The viral prodrome (abdominal pain, vomiting, low-grade fever) preceding neuropsychiatric symptoms is consistent with a viral trigger for autoimmune encephalitis
HSV encephalitis must be empirically treated with acyclovir until PCR results are available due to the devastating consequences of delayed treatment
Male patients with anti-NMDAR encephalitis are less likely to have an underlying tumor compared to females, but tumor screening is still mandatory
Family history of autoimmune disease (maternal Hashimoto's, grandmother's rheumatoid arthritis) may reflect genetic predisposition to autoimmunity

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward presentation with classic features of anti-NMDAR encephalitis including new-onset seizures, orofacial dyskinesias, autonomic instability, extreme delta brush on EEG, and bilateral hippocampal MRI changes. The positive anti-NMDAR antibodies in CSF provide definitive confirmation. HHV-7 CSF positivity adds a minor red herring but the overall clinical picture is clear.
